segments.s185-g33: به پیش + NP is a destination phrase: به پیش here means 'to/toward the presence of' and precedes the noun سپاه برادر ('the army of the brother'). The verb برو is an imperative 'go' placed at the end in typical Persian order; the whole segment is an instruction to move to the brother's host.
segments.s185-g34: تو ... باش is an imperative clause: تو 'you' appears before the predicate noun phrase کینخواه نو ('new avenger'), and باش is the imperative 'be'. The coordinator و links a second clause in which the verb is omitted and understood (elliptical): او شاه نو means 'and let him be the new king', with او as subject and شاه نو as predicate.
segments.s185-g35: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause and زیبد is an impersonal/third-person present meaning 'it befits / it is fitting'. کز is the contracted form meaning 'from/that because of', linking to این غم ('this grief') as the cause; بنالد is a present subjunctive/optative 'should lament' (verb before subject in poetic word order), with پلنگ as the logical subject.
segments.s185-g36: ز + NP is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from/ out of the roaring sea' (ز = from). خروشان is a postpositive participial adjective modifying دریا. برآید is a third-person present/epistolary-future-like verb 'arises/appears' and نهنگ (subject) follows it in inverted poetic order.
segments.s185-g37: وگر is a conditional 'if'. The noun phrase مرغ با ماهیان places 'bird' with the companion phrase با ماهیان ('with the fishes'); اندر آب is a locative 'in the water' that follows the subjects, a common flexible order in poetic Persian.
segments.s185-g38: بخوانند is a present/subjunctive form meaning 'they would sing/recite' used here in a conditional sense. نفرین is the direct object 'a curse', and به + proper name marks the target ('upon Afrasiab'). The verb comes before its object in this poetic clause.
segments.s185-g39: که introduces another explanatory/relative clause. اندر جهان ('in the world') is a locative phrase, followed by چون سیاوش ('like Siyâvash') which qualifies the following noun سوار ('rider/horseman'); the whole phrase sets up a comparison: 'in the world, like Siyâvash the rider...'.
segments.s185-g40: نبندد is the present (poetic) third-person form of 'to bind/gird' (here understood with a negative sense in context: 'does not gird' → 'no one girds'). کمر means 'girdle/waist' (the thing being bound). نیز یک نامدار ('not even one famed man' in this idiomatic arrangement) places نیز 'also/even' with نامدار 'renowned', so the clause says that not even a single famed man girds his waist like Siyâvash.
segments.s185-g9: فسیله (an opening/passage or ‘interval’) is the noun-object here; ببند is an imperative/jussive form meaning “close/shut”; اندر introduces the location “in/within”; آرند is a 3rd-person plural verb form (classical) meaning “they bring/bring in” and نیز “also” links this action to what follows. Together the chunk says roughly “(They) also shut/closed the passages and brought (them) in.”
segments.s185-g10: نماند is a past/resultative verb meaning “remained / was left (there)”; ایچ is the existential negative “nothing/not at all” and follows the verb in typical classical word order; بر کوه و بر دشت are two prepositional phrases (“on the mountain and on the plain”) that specify the location of the non‑existence; چیز is the general noun “thing.” The whole clause reads “nothing remained on mountain or plain.”
segments.s185-g11: همه (“everyone/all”) is the subject; پاک here functions adverbially “entirely/wholly”; سوی + (سپد کوه) gives the direction “toward the white mountain”; برد in this poetic context is a past/telic verb meaning “carried/was taken/driven (away)” (note: the provided noun senses do not list the verbal use, but here it functions as verb). The phrase means “all were wholly driven toward the white mountain.”
segments.s185-g12: ببند here is an instruction/statement “they shut/closed”; اندرون “inside/within” marks the place; سوی + انبوه gives the motion’s target “toward the crowd/throng (انبوه = dense/many)”; برد again is used verbally “carried/drove (into).” Combined: “they shut up the interior and drove (them) into the dense mass.”
segments.s185-g13: جریره names a small place/islet; زنی “a woman” is the predicate noun; بود is the past copula “was”; مام here means “wet‑nurse/nurse” (not merely ‘mother’); فرود functions as a proper name (the nurse associated with Farud). Word order is noun phrase – predicate – copula, typical of narrative: “On Jaryreh there was a woman, the nurse of Farud.”
segments.s185-g14: ز بهر = “because of / for the sake of”; سیاوش is the personal name Siavash; دلش is “her heart” (دل + clitic ش = her); پر ز دود is a predicative construction meaning “full of smoke” (پر = full (of), ز = of/from, دود = smoke). The clause literally: “Because of Siavash her heart was full of smoke,” a poetic way to say she was grief‑struck about Siavash.
segments.s185-g15: بر here is used in the sense “to/toward” (classical usage); مادر is “mother”; آمد is the past tense “came”; فرود جوان is the subject following the verb (typical poetic inversion) and names the speaker “Farud the young.” The phrase: “Farud the young came to the mother.”
segments.s185-g16: بدو گفت is a common sequence meaning “he said to her” (بد = to + indirect object; گفت = said); کای is the vocative particle “O” used to address someone; مام here is vocative “Mam” (the nurse/mother); روشن‌روان is an epithet “bright‑souled” used as a term of address. The chunk reports his direct address: “He said to her, ‘O Mam, bright‑souled.’”
segments.s185-g41: This is a prepositional list introduced by the preposition به (be, “in/for”), enumerating qualities. Word order is noun (نژاد) and qualities in a paratactic series joined by و. گردی here is a poetic noun in the list (see senses). مردی is an abstract noun ‘manliness’; جنگ is the noun ‘war/battle’ and نژاد ‘lineage’. The whole segment functions as the first half of a long enumerative phrase.
segments.s185-g42: Continuation of the same enumerative construction started with به: a sequence of nouns joined by و. باورنگ and فرهنگ are abstract/ornamental nouns (‘matching color / harmonious coloring’ and ‘culture’). سنگ here denotes precious stones/gems in this context, and بداد closes the list; the items are simply juxtaposed by ו with no additional grammatical marking.
segments.s185-g43: Word order here is somewhat inverted but straightforward: بدو (be + 3rd-sg. object) = ‘to him’, داد is the past tense verb ‘gave’, and پیران is the subject ‘the elders’. The clitic-like element م ... را marks the direct object (‘me’), and از نخست ‘from the first / from the beginning’ is a temporal/adverbial phrase modifying the giving. Overall: ‘the elders gave me to him from the first.’
segments.s185-g44: وگر نه is a conditional (‘if not / otherwise’). ز ترکان (‘from the Turks’) is a prepositional phrase; همی is an older Persian modal/emphatic particle giving an ongoing or emphatic sense to the verb. زن here is a noun ‘woman’/‘wife’, and نجست is a past-tense verb meaning ‘sought/searched (for)’ — so the clause means ‘otherwise he would not have sought a woman from the Turks.’
segments.s185-g45: نژاد تو places the noun ‘lineage’ before the possessive pronoun تو (postposed possessor, common in Persian poetry) = ‘your lineage’. از مادر و از پدر are parallel prepositional phrases specifying origin (‘from your mother and from your father’). The whole is a simple genitive description.
segments.s185-g46: A coordinated predicate describing that lineage: همه تاجدار و همه نامور. همه repeated for emphasis (‘all’), تاجدار is an adjective/participial compound ‘crowned’ (literally ‘having a crown’), and نامور ‘renowned’. The construction is terse and typical of epic diction.
segments.s185-g47: An address and apposition: تو پور ‘you, son’ (پور is a vocative/appellative ‘son/descendant’). چنان نامور مهتری is a compressed epithet: چنان (‘so/thus’) modifies نامور (‘renowned’), and مهتری (‘preeminent one / chief’) functions as a head noun — ‘so famed a chief.’ Word order is typical of Persian poetry where adjectives and epithets cluster tightly.
segments.s185-g48: A genitive provenance phrase introduced by ز (‘from/of’): تخم here means ‘seed / offspring’ = ‘descended from,’ followed by the names/epithets کیانی (the Kayanian dynasty) and کی‌منظری (a proper-name or epithet). The whole means ‘of the seed of the Kayanians and Ki-Manzari.’
segments.s185-g17: از ایران سپاه آمد و پیل و کوس — A normal Old/poetic Persian word order: preposition + source (از ایران) followed by the subject and verb (سپاه آمد, 'an army came'). The conjunction و links the following nouns (پیل و کوس, 'elephants and drums') as accompanying elements rather than a separate clause; the verb آمد (3rd sg. past) governs the whole coordinated phrase.
segments.s185-g18: بپیش سپه در سرافراز طوس — بپیش ('ahead of / before') takes سپه ('the host/army') as its object. The remainder (در سرافراز طوس) is an appositive/locative phrase describing who stood there: سرافراز is an epithet 'high‑honored/ proud' modifying the proper name طوس (Tus). The clause omits an explicit verb here (ellipsis): the previous آمد supplies the action (i.e. elephants/drums moved ahead, and Tus stood proudly at the front).
segments.s185-g19: چه گویی چه باید کنون ساختن — دو پرسشِ کوتاه: چه گویی is an interrogative phrase 'what do you say?' with چه (what) + گویی (you say; 2nd person present/poetic). The second clause uses the impersonal modal باید ('must/ought') + کنون ('now') and the infinitive ساختن ('to do/make') to mean 'what ought (to be) done now?'.
segments.s185-g20: نباید که آرد یکی تاختن — نباید is an impersonal negative modal 'it must not / one must not.' The conjunction که introduces the subordinate clause; آرد here is the subjunctive/optative form of آورد ('bring'/'bring about'), so که آرد یکی تاختن = 'that someone should bring about an attack/charge.' یکی is an indefinite 'someone,' and تاختن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to charge/attack.' Together: 'There must not be anyone who charges.'
segments.s185-g21: جریره بدو گفت کای رزمساز — جریره is the speaker's name; بدو is the dative enclitic/phrase 'to him.' گفت is the past tense verb 'said.' کای is the vocative particle 'O' and رزمساز is an epithet 'war‑maker' meaning 'warrior/commander' addressed to the addressee.
segments.s185-g22: بدین روز هرگز مبادت نیاز — بدین روز is a temporal phrase 'on this day' (بـ + این روز). هرگز 'never' modifies the optative/negative wish مبادت (from مباد, 'may it not be'); مبادت is a jussive/optative meaning 'may it not happen' or 'may there not be.' نیاز means 'need' or 'want/supplication.' So the line prays 'may you never have need on this day.'
segments.s185-g23: بایران برادرت شاه نوست — بایران here functions as the locative 'in Iran' (poetic/contracted form of به ایران). برادرت is 'your brother' (برادر + 2nd‑person suffix -ت). شاه نوست is an adjectival/copular phrase: شاه 'king' + نوست ('is new' / 'new is') yielding 'is the new king' — classic Persian sometimes places the copular notion inside the adjective.
segments.s185-g24: جهاندار و بیدار کیخسروست — جهاندار 'world‑holder / sovereign' and بیدار 'awake, vigilant' are two epithets applied to the proper name that follows; کیخسروست is 'is Kay Khosrow' (the full predicate identifies the sovereign as Kay Khosrow). The -ست ending is the copula 'is' attached to the name.
segments.s185-g73: سپه را (spear with را) is fronted for emphasis/definiteness; را marks سپه as definite/object. باش is the imperative 'be' addressed to تو (you); این زمان پیش رو is an adverbial phrase 'at this time, forward/in front' with پیش رو (compound) meaning 'ahead' or 'in the vanguard'. The word order is poetic: object (سپه را) + imperative + temporal/adverbial phrase.
segments.s185-g74: تویی is the emphatic/stressed copula 'thou art' (present). کینه‌خواه is a noun 'avenger' (one who seeks vengeance). جهاندار is a title 'world-ruler/sovereign.' نو modifies جهاندار as 'new' or 'fresh'; the three nouns form a predicative nominal group identifying the addressee.
segments.s185-g75: ترا (here segmented as ت + را) is the accusative/oblique pronoun 'thee' placed before the impersonal/modal predicate. باید is the impersonal/modal 'it is necessary / must'. پیش is adverbial 'first / before (this)'. بکین (poetic form) + ساختن form a compound infinitive meaning 'to effect/take vengeance' (بکین = 'do/avenge', ساختن as a light verb 'make/bring about'). Word order places the object pronoun before the necessity + infinitive clause in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s185-g76: کمر بر میان بستن is an idiomatic infinitive phrase 'to bind the waist around the middle' = 'gird the loins'; بر is the preposition 'on/round'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. تاختن is an infinitive meaning 'to charge / to gallop', so the two infinitives give two successive battle-preparatory commands.
segments.s185-g77: بدو is a dative phrase 'to him / unto him'. گفت is the past verb 'said' (here introducing reported speech); رای is the noun 'counsel/plan' and functions as the object/theme of the reported utterance. تو‌ای (vocative) 'you, O' plus شیر زن (compound 'lion-woman' = 'lion-hearted woman' or 'valiant woman') addresses the addressee. Word order follows the classical pattern of 'to him said: "your counsel, O ..."'.
segments.s185-g78: درفشان is either a noun 'standard-bearer' or an epithet 'the flag‑waver/shining one' and is the subject; کند is the present/past light verb 'does/makes' used to form the predicate. دوده 'smoke' and انجمن 'assembly/host' form the objects coordinated by و — the phrase says the standard‑bearer causes smoke (to rise among) the assembly/host. The clause completes the reported counsel.
segments.s185-g79: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. برخاست is the past verb 'rose/was raised'. آوای 'the sound/voice' is the subject, and کوس is the instrument 'war‑drum'. از + چرم 'from the hide' indicates the drum's skin as the source; the clause describes the drum's sound arising from the stretched hide.
segments.s185-g80: جهان is the patient 'the world' (object/affected domain). کرد is the causative/past 'made/turned'. چون آبنوس is a simile 'like ebony' describing the world’s changed appearance. از introduces the source or cause 'from', and می‌م here is an onomatopoetic or poetic element (not a standard lexical item) tied to the drum‑sound; its precise lexical sense is archaic/poetic and not represented in the provided sense list.
segments.s185-g1: پس is a temporal/conclusive adverb at the start ('then'). The clause has a simple order: subject آگاهی (news/information) + verb آمد (came) followed by a prepositional/locative phrase بنزد فرود ('to the presence of Farud'); بنزد is an older form meaning 'in the presence of' or 'to'. فرود here is used as the addressee (a proper name).
segments.s185-g2: که introduces a result/relative clause ('that/so that'); شد is the past of شدن meaning 'became'. The noun روی 'face' is the grammatical subject and is followed by modifiers خورشید تابان ('the shining sun') used in a simile/metaphor, and the predicate adjective کبود ('dusky/dark') completes the clause.
segments.s185-g3: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. The phrase is a genitive/compound: نعل ستوران = 'the horseshoes of the steeds'. The sequence وز پای پیل continues the same idea: وز here functions as an archaic conjunction equivalent to 'and/from' linking the following part, پای پیل = 'the elephant’s foot'.
segments.s185-g4: جهان is the subject 'the world' and شد is the copular verb 'became'. The predicate is the nominal phrase بکردار دریای نیل: بکردار is an attributive/old-fashioned adjective-like word and دریای نیل is a genitive 'the sea of the Nile' used metaphorically — together expressing that the world was transformed into or resembled a Nile‑like sea.
segments.s185-g5: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. ناکار دیده جوان is a compact noun phrase: ناکار ('ill‑fated/ineffectual') modifies دیده ('seen/eye' used here attributively), which in turn characterizes جوان ('the young man')—together referring to the young man afflicted by or who had seen misfortune.
segments.s185-g6: دلش = دل (heart) + enclitic ش ('his') shows possession. گشت is the past of گشتن used here as 'became'. پر before a noun means 'full (of)', so پر درد = 'full of pain'. The conjunction و links the next nominal phrase تیره روان, where تیره ('gloomy/dark') modifies روان ('spirit/soul').
segments.s185-g7: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past-tense verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces the subordinate content/purpose clause ('that/so that'). هرچ ('whatever') is the indefinite object; بودش is بود (was/existed) with the enclitic ش referring back to 'it/that', and یله is the predicate/adjective 'loose/unrestrained' describing what should be done to those things.
segments.s185-g8: هیونان is a plural noun 'beasts/animals'. وز again functions as an archaic conjunction 'and' linking two groups. گوسفندان گله is a genitive/appositive phrase: گوسفندان 'sheep' followed by گله 'flock/herd' — together 'the flock of sheep' or simply 'the flocks of sheep'.
segments.s185-g81: یکی is an indefinite premodifier placed before the noun (here: 'a certain' / 'a') rather than the numeral 'one'. دیده‌بان (sentinel) is the subject; آمد is the simple past 3rd‑person verb 'came'. از دیده‌گاه is a prepositional phrase ('from the lookout/viewpoint') with از marking origin.
segments.s185-g82: سخن گفت is a common collocation where the noun سخن ('speech/words') plus گفت (past of گفتن) yields 'spoke' or 'addressed'. با او ('with/to him') marks the addressee. ز ایران سپاه is a prepositional/genitive phrase introduced by ز (poetic 'of/about/from') meaning 'about/of the army of Iran' and functions as the topic/object of the speaking verb.
segments.s185-g83: که here is the conjunction 'that' introducing reported content. The coordinate list دشت و در و کوه ('plain and gate/valley and mountain') is the locus, followed by پر + لشکرست: پر 'full (of)' attaches to the following predicate and لشکرست is an archaic fused predication ('is a host' / 'there is a host'), so the whole means 'that plain, gate and mountain are full of troops.'
segments.s185-g84: تو is the 2nd‑person subject/vocative 'you'. خورشید is literal 'sun' used here vocatively or metaphorically. گویی (here) functions as a finite verb/particle meaning 'you say' / 'you declare' (an archaic/poetic present). ببند is the 2nd‑person imperative 'close!' and اندرست is a verbless predicate 'it is inside' (اندر + archaic copula), so together this reports speech: 'you say, "Close it — it is inside."'
segments.s185-g85: ز marks origin/extent 'from'. دربند here is used as a place-name ('Darbend' / the pass/fort), followed by دژ 'fortress'. تا is the 'as far as'/extent preposition connecting the range, بیابان 'wilderness/plain' gives the other boundary, and گنگ qualifies the scene as 'muffled/indistinct' or 'distant', modifying the phrase.
segments.s185-g86: سپاهست is an archaic fused predication meaning 'it is an army / there is a host' (سپاه + است). The following و links the enumeration 'and elephants and men of war' (پیلان 'elephants', مردان 'men/warriors'); جنگ qualifies مردان as 'men of war' (i.e. warriors).
segments.s185-g87: فرود here denotes a downward movement or 'descent' (they came down). از در دژ is the origin 'from the gate of the fortress'. فرو is a reinforcing adverb 'down(ward)'. هشت بند is a numeral + measure phrase 'eight bands/detachments' (بند used as a segment/unit here), indicating how many descended.
segments.s185-g88: نگه + کرد is a light‑verb construction: نگه (gaze/watch) + کرد 'did/made' = 'kept watch' or 'looked on'. لشکر is the subject 'the army'; ز کوه بلند 'from the high mountain' gives the vantage point (ز = 'from', کوه بلند = 'high mountain').
segments.s185-g97: جوان is the subject 'the young man'; با + تخوار is a prepositional phrase 'with the wretch' placed after the subject. سرایند is the verb 'to compose/sing' in an archaic/poetic third‑person plural form, and گفت is the reporting verb 'said' introducing the following clause. Word order is Subject — (with X) — verb — reporting verb.
segments.s185-g98: که introduces reported speech ('that'); هر چت is the fused phrase هر + چه + ت (هر چه + ـت) meaning 'whatever (that) you/that you to …' with ت the 2nd‑person enclitic attached to چ. بپرسم is a first‑person present/subjunctive form 'I may ask / I ask' used inside the subordinate clause, and نباید نهفت is a modal construction: نباید 'must not/should not' + نهفت (participle/adjective 'hidden') meaning 'must not be hidden.'
segments.s185-g99: کنارنگ is a preposed locative/epithet ('at the side/at the flank') qualifying the scene. وز here functions as a poetic connective (not a literal ’gust’); هرک 'whoever' is the indefinite relative, and دارد درفش is a present tense clause 'has/holds a standard' (دارد = 'has', درفش = 'banner/standard').
segments.s185-g100: خداوند is an honorific/title 'lord' placed before the name گوپال (here a proper name). و links the next phrase; زرینه is an adjective 'golden' that modifies کفش 'shoes/boots' — together describing the lord's equipment. Word order is Title + Name + and + adjective + noun.
segments.s185-g101: چو بینی is a temporal/conditional clause 'when you see' (چو = 'when', بینی = 2nd‑person singular 'you see'). به من is the indirect object 'to me', نام ایشان 'their name(s)', and بگوی is a subjunctive/imperative form 'say / tell' addressed to the same 'you' — an exhortation to report the names to the speaker.
segments.s185-g102: کسی را 'someone' is marked with را (definite/object marker) because it is the object demanded by the previous verb ('tell me someone'). که introduces a relative clause 'whom/that'; دانی is 2nd‑person present 'you know', and از ایران بروی completes the relative clause 'to have come from Iran' (از = 'from', ایران = 'Iran', بروی here functions as the verb of motion/coming in the relative clause).
segments.s185-g57: بدو گفت is a short clause meaning “he said to him.” بدو is the dative/enclitic-style form meaning “to him/to you” placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت is the reporting verb (“said”). ز ایدر برو is a sequence of prepositional phrase + imperative: ز ایدر = “from here/ hence,” and برو is the imperative “go.” با + noun (تخوار) indicates accompaniment or relation (“with/among the wretch”).
segments.s185-g58: مدار here functions as an imperative/admonitive verb in this poetic context meaning roughly “do not let / do not consider” (the printed lemma senses did not show this verbal use). این سخن is the direct object phrase “this speech/word.” بر دل خویش is a prepositional phrase “upon your heart,” where خویش is split orthographically into خوی + ش, the latter (ش) being the enclitic possessive “his/your/one’s.” خوار is a predicate adjective “despised/abased,” describing the state one should not let the heart fall into.
segments.s185-g59: کز here is the subordinating/relative particle “that/for/since.” ایران is the place noun. که is the relative/introducing particle “who/that.” و is the simple conjunction “and/then.” مه in this poetic line functions as “moon/one who knows the moon” (an idiomatic stock word in classical Persian); شناسد is the finite verb “knows/recognizes” (the provided sense list did not include the verbal sense, so the mapping is left undefined), and همه is the quantifier “all/everyone.” Together the clause means roughly “that in Iran whoever recognizes (him) — everyone…”
segments.s185-g60: بگوید here is a present/future-reporting verb “they say / will call” (the lemma senses given do not show the verbal sense). نشان is the noun “sign/token.” شبان is “shepherd” and رمه is “flock; herd”; the conjunction و links them. The verb governs نشان as its object: “they call (him) the sign of shepherd and flock.”
segments.s185-g61: This is a list introduced by the preposition ز ‘from’ (ز بهرام = “from Bahram”). The next item is written وز which here functions as the fused form of و + ز (“and from”). زنگه is a proper name (Zangah) in this context, and شاوران (شورَوان) is a plural noun “counselors/advisers.” The construction enumerates sources “from Bahram and from Zangah, the advisers.”
segments.s185-g62: نشان is again “token/sign.” جو here is the verbal root meaning “seek/look for” (the provided senses listed only an unrelated noun ‘barley,’ so the mapping is -1); ز introduces the source “from,” and گردان is “troop/regiment.” و joins جنگ‌آوران “warriors.” Grammatically the clause is parallel to the previous: “seek a token from the regiments and the warriors.”
segments.s185-g63: همیشه is an adverb “always/ever.” سر و نام تو is a coordinated noun phrase “your head and your name,” where تو is the 2nd-person singular pronoun following the noun phrase. زنده‌باد is an optative formula “long live / be alive” (a fused wish with باد as the optative particle). Word order places the wished-for items before the formula.
segments.s185-g64: روان here is the noun “soul/spirit.” سیاوش is the proper name Siavash. فروزنده is an adjective/epithet “radiant/illuminating” modifying روان, and باد is the optative particle “may” used to form the wish (as in زنده‌باد). Together this is another wish: “May Siavash’s soul be radiant.”
segments.s185-g89: وزان پس is a literary phrase meaning 'after that' (وز + پس = then/after). بیامد is the simple past third-person singular 'he came'. در دژ is a locative phrase 'into/within the fortress' placed before the final verb ببست, which is the past tense 'he shut/closed' (the object is omitted but understood). The sequence shows classic Persian SOV tendencies but with poetic word order.
segments.s185-g90: یکی باره is an idiomatic compound 'one time/at once' = 'suddenly'. تیز رو is a compound adjective meaning 'swift-moving' (تیز = swift); رو here is a bound element making a verbal/vestigial adjective rather than the separate prepositional sense. بر is the preposition 'upon/on' and نشست is the past tense 'he sat' or 'alighted' (third singular).
segments.s185-g91: برفتند is the simple past plural 'they went/they departed'. پویان here is most plausibly a proper name (or an epithet used as a name) and is therefore treated as a name. تخوار and فرود in this line function as personal names as well; the coordinating و simply links them.
segments.s185-g92: جوان را uses را as the topical/definite-marking particle, marking 'the young man' as the phrase being commented on. سر بخت is a compound where سر='head' and بخت='fate/fortune' — idiomatically 'fortune's head' = 'good fortune' — and بر گرد بود literally 'was around' (بر گرد = around; بود = past 'was'), so the clause means 'fortune was around the young man's head' (i.e. he was favored by fate).
segments.s185-g93: از افراز means 'from the heights' (از = from; افراز = high place/height). چون here is the temporal/concessive 'when/as', کژ = 'crooked/askew', and گردد (گَردد) is the present/subjunctive/quotative third-person 'becomes/is turned' — a common poetic verb form. سپهر 'sky/heaven' appears at the end as the subject in inverted poetic order.
segments.s185-g94: نه ... نه is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor'. تندی بکار آید is an idiom meaning 'haste/violence will be of use' (تندی = haste/violence; بکار آید = "be of use/avail"). از بن = 'from the root/from the foundation' and نه مهر negates 'love/kindness' as also failing to help. The verbs are impersonal/predictive (آید = 'will come/happen' or 'avail').
segments.s185-g95: گزیدند is the past plural 'they chose'. تیغ here is used in a topographical, figurative sense 'a blade/point' = 'ridge/peak' rather than a literal sword. یکی precedes the noun group as 'a certain/one', and برز کوه is a poetic inversion meaning 'high mountain' (برز = high, کوه = mountain).
segments.s185-g96: که introduces a relative/clerical clause 'from which/so that'. دیدار here functions as 'sight/view' (used poetically to mean 'could be seen'), a sense not listed in the provided options, so it's marked -1. بد is an adjective 'bad/striking' (here shading toward 'formidable' or 'striking to the eye'), یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once', and ایران گروه is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the troop/host of Iran' (word order inverted for poetic effect).
segments.s185-g65: ازین (از + این) is the prepositional phrase 'from/of these'; هر دو (هر + دو is compressed here) means 'both/these two'. هرگز is the adverb 'never' and precedes the verb نگشتی (past of گشتن) to negate it: 'never became'. جدای is an adjective/noun 'separate' that completes the verb phrase 'never became separate'. Word order is topical prepositional phrase → adverb → verb → predicative adjective.
segments.s185-g66: کنارَنگ (here written as one word) means 'at the side / beside'; بودند is the past plural of بودن 'they were' describing location/state. و is the coordinating 'and'; او is the third‑person pronoun 'he'; پادشای is the poetic form of 'پادشا/پادشاه' meaning 'king'. The clause therefore states a parallel result: 'they were beside (him) and he was king.'
segments.s185-g67: نشان (a 'sign' or 'token') is the object of the following verb. خواه is the present/imperative stem of خواستن here used elliptically (classical poetry often drops the initial ب of the imperative), so نشان خواه = 'ask/seek a sign'. ازین دو = 'from these two' (از + این). گو is the imperative 'say' addressed to the addressee; سرفراز is a vocative 'O noble/honored one'. Word order: object + imperative verb + source phrase, then a second imperative 'say' + vocative.
segments.s185-g68: کز is the relative/conjunction 'that/for which/from which'. ایشان = 'they/them'. The tokens shown (م + را) together form مرا 'me' (here split in the token list); و is 'and'; ت + را correspond to ترا 'you' with the object marker (t = 2nd‑person enclitic, را = object marker). نیست is the negative copula 'is not/there is not'; راز is 'secret'. The whole phrase means 'for/from them there is no secret between you and me' (i.e. 'they hide nothing from us').
segments.s185-g69: سران را and گردنکشان را are two definite objects marked by را ('the chiefs' and 'the proud/rebels'). بخوان is an imperative (بخوان) from خواندن; in this epic context it functions as 'call/summon' or 'invite' (imperative addressing a commander or court‑officer). The object(s) precede the verb as is common in Persian.
segments.s185-g70: می‌و (میوه 'fruit') is a noun meaning 'fruit' (often used for provisions). خلعت is 'robe(s) of honour' and آرای here is 'array/ornament' (to adorn). و بالا و خوان links further items: بالا 'high/lofty' (qualifying the following) and خوان 'banquet/table'. The sequence lists things to be prepared for the summoned: fruit, robes of honour (to dress/ornament them), and the high feast.
segments.s185-g71: ز گیتی = 'from/of the world' (ز is a poetic preposition). برادر + ت + را is the sequence 'برادرت را' (your brother as definite object: برادر + 2nd‑person suffix ت + object marker را). گنج بس = 'treasure is enough' (بس = 'enough'). The phrase means 'From the world's wealth, treasure for your brother is sufficient.'
segments.s185-g72: همان 'the same/that very' points back to the treatment prescribed; کین 'vengeance/hatred' and آیین 'custom/law/rite' are paired as the actions or norms to be observed. به introduces the target 'بیگانه کس' — 'a foreign person/stranger'. The clause says roughly that 'the same vengeance and customary rule (apply) to strangers' — the exact force is idiomatic: these are the measures to be held regarding foreigners.
segments.s185-g49: کمربست is a poetic past 3rd-person singular ('he girded [his] belt' / 'he prepared himself'). باید is a modal 'must/ought' that scopes over the following verb بکین. بکین is a poetic present/subjunctive/imperative form (here meaning 'avenge' / 'take vengeance') and takes پدر as its direct object (no را is used). The subject ('he') is implied from کمربست.
segments.s185-g50: بجای آوردن is an infinitival/periphrastic expression meaning 'to bring back / to restore' (بجای 'in the place' + آوردن 'to bring'); here it expresses purpose or result connected to بکین. نژاد و گهر are coordinated direct objects ('lineage and jewel/gem'), the second joined by و. The whole segment states what must be restored as part of the action.
segments.s185-g51: چنین ('thus') modifies گفت, which is the past verb 'he said' (the provided sense list did not include this verbal sense). ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that / thereupon'. بمادر ('to his mother') uses the old prepositional prefix بـ- + مادر, and فرود (literally 'descent' / 'alighting') is used as a verbal noun indicating he went down/approached her.
segments.s185-g52: کز introduces a source/partitive idea here ('from / of'), so کز ایران سخن = 'of Iran's speech/tales'. با که باید سرود is a question-like clause: با 'with/about', که 'who/whom', باید 'must', and سرود is the verb/noun 'to sing/compose an ode' (the provided senses do not exactly match the verbal use). Together: 'about whom (among Iran's tales) must one sing?'
segments.s185-g53: The initial که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). باید is the modal 'must' and the second که plus باشد form the subordinate predication 'that there be...' — این ترکیب ('باید که باشد') is the idiomatic 'it is necessary that ... be'. م + را is an orthographic split of مرا ('me' as object); پایمرد is the noun 'steadfastness/endurance' serving as the thing that should 'be to me' (i.e. 'I must have steadfastness').
segments.s185-g54: ازین is 'from these / of these' (از + این in older spelling). سرفرازان is the plural noun 'the proud / honored (ones)' — here 'proud champions/warriors'. روز نبرد is a temporal/place phrase 'day of battle' (= 'on the battlefield'); the whole segment specifies the adversaries and the context of fighting.
segments.s185-g55: کز ایشان = 'from/of them / among them'. ندانم is the negative present 'I do not know'. کسی را بنام = 'anyone by name' (کسی را object marker را + بنام 'called / by name'); the phrase means 'I do not know anyone by name among them.'
segments.s185-g56: نیامد is past negative 'did not come'. بر من = 'to me' (بر used as 'toward/to' + pronoun من). درود و پیام are the coordinated nouns 'greeting and message' — none of these arrived to the speaker.
segments.s185-g25: ترا is the object form (ت + را) 'you' placed before the verb (classical Persian allows object-before-verb). نیک is adverbial here ('well/ favorably') modifying the verb داند, which is an older classical 3rd-person present 'they know / regard'. به + noun (به نام و گهر) marks the respect/instrument ('by/through your name and gem') and follows the verb phrase.
segments.s185-g26: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. هم‌خون is a noun/adjective 'of the same blood'. وز is a contracted sequence (و + ز) meaning 'and from', printed as one word in the poem (none of the listed senses fit exactly, so marked separately). مهره یک پدر is a figurative phrase: مهره 'bead/ornament' used metonymically for offspring or a 'piece' of the father's line; یک پدر = 'of one father' (=same father).
segments.s185-g27: برادرت‌گر is a fused sequence: برادرت 'your brother' + conditional گر 'if' (classical enclitic conditional). کینه جوید is a verbal phrase: کینه (hatred/grudge) with جوید (classical present 'seeks/harbors'); همی is the persistent/continuous particle ('still/constantly' or simply emphatic here). The order places the conditional noun-clause before the verb, typical in Ferdowsi.
segments.s185-g28: روان is 'spirit/soul' and functions as the subject of the clause. سیاوش is the proper name; بشوید is an older 3rd-person-form of شدن ('become' / 'be set in motion') used here in a future/inchoative sense. همی again adds continuity/emphasis. The verb follows the subject (subject–verb order).
segments.s185-g29: گر 'if' opens the protasis; او 'he' is the subject. کینه جوید is the verb phrase 'harbors/seeks a grudge' (جوید = present/classical 'seeks/harbors'), and همی is the emphatic/continuous particle. از نیا ('because of/from ancestry') is a short prepositional phrase giving the cause or ground of the grudge.
segments.s185-g30: ترا را is the marked person phrase 'toward you / concerning you' (ت + را as before). کینه is 'hatred/grudge' as the topic/predicate. زیباتر is the comparative form of زیبا ('more beautiful / more noble') describing that grudge; و links the simile, and کیمیا (kīmiyā) is used metaphorically ('elixir' / precious, wondrous thing). Note classical word order: the marked object/person (ترا را) comes early, and predicate adjectives follow.
segments.s185-g31: برت را is an object phrase ('your covering / your mantle' or 'upon you' dependent on context). بخفتان here is a poetic/archaic descriptor of a garment or fabric (the listed senses don't match exactly in the provided lemma list, so the specific lexical sense is irregular in the modern gloss). رومی qualifies that fabric ('Roman/Byzantine'), and بپوش is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'put on / wear'. The sequence is an imperative clause: object + description + verb.
segments.s185-g32: برو is the imperative 'go'. دل پر از جوش is a noun phrase 'a heart full of još' where جوش here means 'ardor / passionate surge' and پر is the adjective 'full (of)'. سر پر خروش is a parallel noun phrase 'a head full of tumult/uproar' (سر 'head', خروش 'tumult'). The two noun phrases are linked by و and describe the mental/emotional state with which the addressee should go.

sentences.s185-s17: Go before your brother's army; you be the new avenger and he the new king.
sentences.s185-s18: For it is fitting that, because of this grief, the panther should lament, and from the roaring sea a whale should arise.
sentences.s185-s19: If birds together with the fishes in the water were to sing, they would curse Afrasiab.
sentences.s185-s20: that in the world no famed man, like Siyâvash the horseman, girds his waist.
sentences.s185-s5: They shut up the openings and brought them inside as well; nothing at all remained on mountain or plain.
sentences.s185-s6: All, wholly, were driven toward the white mountain; they shut up the interior and drove them into the dense throng.
sentences.s185-s7: On the islet Jaryreh there was a woman, the wet‑nurse of Farud; because of Siavash her heart was full of smoke.
sentences.s185-s8: Farud the young came to the mother; he said to her, “O Mam, bright‑souled.”
sentences.s185-s21: In bravery and manliness, in battle and noble lineage; in matching color, culture, gems, and justice.
sentences.s185-s22: The elders gave me to him from the first; otherwise he would not have sought a woman among the Turks.
sentences.s185-s23: Your lineage from mother and from father — all crowned and all renowned.
sentences.s185-s24: You, son — so famed a chief, of the seed of the Kayanians and (of) Ki-Manzari.
sentences.s185-s9: An army came from Iran, with elephants and war‑drums ahead of the host, and the proud Tus at its forefront.
sentences.s185-s10: What do you say? What must be done now? One must not let anyone make a charge.
sentences.s185-s11: Jarirah said to him, 'O warrior, may you never be in need on this day.'
sentences.s185-s12: In Iran your brother is the new king; Kay Khosrow is sovereign and vigilant.
sentences.s185-s37: Be at the head of the army at this time; you are the avenger, a new sovereign.
sentences.s185-s38: You must first exact vengeance; gird your loins and charge.
sentences.s185-s39: They said to him, 'Your counsel, O lion‑woman: the standard‑bearer will scatter smoke over the host.'
sentences.s185-s40: When the sound of the war‑drum rose from the hide, it made the world like ebony from its 'mīm'.
sentences.s185-s1: Then news came to Farud, and his face became dark like the shining sun.
sentences.s185-s2: From the horseshoes of the steeds and from the elephant’s feet, the world became like a Nile‑sea.
sentences.s185-s3: When the young man heard of that unlucky thing, his heart became full of pain and his spirit grew dark.
sentences.s185-s4: He ordered that whatever there was be set loose — the beasts and the flocks of sheep.
sentences.s185-s41: A sentinel came from the lookout and spoke with him about the Iranian army.
sentences.s185-s42: For the plain and the gate and the mountain are full of a host; you (then) say, 'Close it — it is inside.'
sentences.s185-s43: From the Darband fortress as far as the wilderness the host stretches — elephants and warriors of war.
sentences.s185-s44: Down from the gate of the fortress came eight detachments; the army kept watch from the high mountain.
sentences.s185-s49: The young man, together with the wretch, sang and said that whatever I ask you must not be concealed.
sentences.s185-s50: At the flank stood whoever bore a standard — Lord Gūpāl, with golden shoes.
sentences.s185-s51: When you see them, tell me their names — someone whom you know to have come from Iran.
sentences.s185-s29: He said to him, “Go hence; do not let this speech abase your heart before the lowly.”
sentences.s185-s30: For in Iran, whoever recognizes (him) — all will call (him) the sign of shepherds and of the flock.
sentences.s185-s31: Seek a token from Bahram and from Zangah, the counselors; seek one from the regiments and the warriors.
sentences.s185-s32: May your head and name live forever; may Siavash’s spirit be luminous.
sentences.s185-s45: After that he entered the fortress and shut it; at once a swift rider sat upon it.
sentences.s185-s46: Puyān, Takhvār, and Farud departed; fortune stood round the young man's head.
sentences.s185-s47: When the heavens are turned askew from above, neither haste nor affection will avail from the root.
sentences.s185-s48: They chose the ridge of a high mountain, from which the whole host of Iran could be seen at once.
sentences.s185-s33: Of these two you never became separate; they stood at his side and he was the king.
sentences.s185-s34: Ask a token of these two — say, 'O honored one, there is no secret between them and you and me.'
sentences.s185-s35: Summon the chiefs and the proud ones; set out fruit, array them with robes of honour, and raise the high banquet.
sentences.s185-s36: From the world, for your brother a treasure is enough; the same — vengeance and custom — (is for) no foreign person.
sentences.s185-s25: He girded his belt; he must avenge his father and restore his lineage and jewel.
sentences.s185-s26: Thus he said; then he went to his mother and asked, 'Of Iran's tales, whom should I sing about?'
sentences.s185-s27: That I must be steadfast against these proud champions on the battlefield.
sentences.s185-s28: Of them I know no one by name; no greeting or message came to me.
sentences.s185-s13: They know you well by name and by your gem; you are of the same blood and of one father's seed.
sentences.s185-s14: If your brother bears a grudge, Siawash's spirit will be stirred.
sentences.s185-s15: If he bears a grudge because of ancestry, your grudge will be more splendid and like an elixir.
sentences.s185-s16: Put on for yourself the Roman brocade; go with a heart full of ardor and a head full of tumult.
